sources.name: Bureau of Labor Statistics (LAUS + CES)
sources.notes: Monthly state and metro labor market + payroll employment series.

sources.name: U.S. Census Bureau Population Estimates (via FRED FLPOP)
sources.notes: Annual Florida resident population (source notes cite U.S. Census Bureau).

sources.name: FRED state innovation/development indicators
sources.notes: Business applications and real gross state product series used for innovation/economic development tab.

narrative.headline: Florida remains large and dynamic, with selective signs to watch.
whatStandsOut: Nonfarm Payrolls are down 20.6k over the last year.
Labor Force is down 43,962 people year-over-year.
improving: Unemployment remains low by historical standards despite a 1.3 point annual increase.
Private Education & Health Services is among the strongest job growers (2.9% YoY).
Manufacturing is among the strongest job growers (0.2% YoY).
Trade, Transportation, Utilities is among the strongest job growers (-0.3% YoY).
whyItMatters: A growing labor force plus payroll expansion signals depth, not just short-term demand spikes.
Sector breadth helps Florida absorb national volatility while keeping opportunity distributed.
Population gains reinforce long-run demand for housing, services, and infrastructure investment.

narrative.headline: Florida’s innovation stack remains large, with selective areas requiring watchfulness.
signals: Professional & business services employment is down 1.0% year-over-year.
development: Real gross state product is up 11.9% over three years.
momentum: Innovation metrics are intended as directional signal layers, not standalone verdicts.
Business formation plus advanced-service employment growth is a strong expansion pattern.
Development capacity and innovation capacity should be tracked together for policy and capital allocation.
